title: Takaichi's Triumph: Reshaping Japanese Politics
content:
As a young woman, Sanae Takaichi took a liking to motorcycles, famously showing up to an admissions interview on her Kawasaki. Now, as Japan’s prime minister, Ms. Takaichi has proven to be a bold risk-taker in the political arena as well. She recently gambled by calling snap elections for the powerful lower house, hoping her personal appeal would lift her less popular Liberal Democratic Party (LDP). The bet paid off spectacularly with the biggest election victory in Japan’s post-war history. This remarkable result, which secured the LDP a commanding two-thirds supermajority, has the potential to reshape Japanese politics for years to come.

Ms. Takaichi, who balances being a fiscal dove with being a security hawk, now wields a massive personal mandate. The LDP, after stumbling in recent years, has returned to a position of unquestioned supremacy. Thanks to its supermajority, the party will be able to control the legislative process and override the more fractious upper house. The scale of this triumph shocked even LDP insiders, as the party won big across every region and age group. With 316 seats secured on its own, the LDP is now scrambling to train a large influx of novice MPs.

Two phenomena largely explain this outcome, the first being Ms. Takaichi’s potent personal popularity. On the campaign trail, she electrified audiences, dragging her traditionally fusty party into the digital era and becoming a social media star. As the country’s first female prime minister, she cuts a welcome contrast to previous leaders with her middle-class upbringing and plain-spoken style. At the same time, she appeals to voters anxious about national security in an increasingly dangerous world. The second factor was the complete collapse of the opposition, as hasty pre-election mergers produced confusion rather than synergy.

With a commanding majority, the LDP can easily bypass most obstacles the opposition attempts to throw up. Ms. Takaichi has declared a clear course to make Japan both stronger and more prosperous. She plans to strengthen Japan’s armed forces in response to China’s rise, accelerating the timeline for increasing defense spending to 2% of GDP. She also hopes to lift restrictions on arms exports to boost the domestic defense industry and favors creating a new national intelligence agency. Ultimately, she may try to achieve the longstanding conservative goal of revising Japan’s pacifist constitution, though this requires overcoming significant hurdles.

Despite her admiration for free-market icons, Ms. Takaichi sees the path to prosperity running heavily through state intervention. She wants to spend significantly on strengthening supply chains and has set out 17 areas for focused industrial policy support. However, her promises to spend big while simultaneously offering households relief from inflation present obvious tensions. Furthermore, her nationalist streak and historical revisionism regarding Japan's imperial past may strain crucial relations with neighbors like China and South Korea. If she can navigate these complex economic and diplomatic challenges, Ms. Takaichi could prove as consequential as she is popular.
